objectives: Conjugate reflexive verbs with correct pronouns (me, te, se, nos, os, se)
Use reflexive verbs for daily routine and personal care
Distinguish reflexive from non-reflexive use of the same verb
Ask and answer questions about daily routines

dialogue_blocks.speaker: Mamá
dialogue_blocks.text: ¡Mija, ya son las siete! ¿A qué hora te despiertas?
dialogue_blocks.translation: Honey, it's already seven! What time do you wake up?
dialogue_blocks.ipa: [ˈmixa ‖ ja son las ˈsjete ‖ a ke ˈoɾa te ðesˈpjeɾtas]

dialogue_blocks.speaker: Valeria
dialogue_blocks.text: Me despierto a las siete y media, mamá. Me levanto ahora.
dialogue_blocks.translation: I wake up at seven thirty, mom. I'm getting up now.
dialogue_blocks.ipa: [me ðesˈpjeɾto a las ˈsjete i ˈmeðja ‖ me leˈβanto aˈoɾa]

dialogue_blocks.speaker: Mamá
dialogue_blocks.translation: How long does it take you to get ready?

dialogue_blocks.speaker: Valeria
dialogue_blocks.text: Me ducho en diez minutos, me visto rápido y me maquillo en el camino.
dialogue_blocks.translation: I shower in ten minutes, dress quickly and do my makeup on the way.
dialogue_blocks.ipa: [me ˈðuʧo en ˈðjeθ miˈnutos ‖ me ˈβisto ˈrapiðo i me maˈkiʝo en el kaˈmino]

dialogue_blocks.speaker: Valeria
dialogue_blocks.text: Sí, él siempre se levanta primero y se baña antes que yo.
dialogue_blocks.translation: Yes, he always gets up first and bathes before me.
dialogue_blocks.ipa: [si ‖ el ˈsjempɾe se leˈβanta pɾiˈmeɾo i se ˈbaɲa ˈantes ke jo]

dialogue_blocks.speaker: Mamá
dialogue_blocks.text: ¿A qué hora se acuestan ustedes normalmente?
dialogue_blocks.translation: What time do you all normally go to bed?
dialogue_blocks.ipa: [a ke ˈoɾa se aˈkwestan usˈteðes noɾmalˈmente]

dialogue_blocks.speaker: Valeria
dialogue_blocks.text: Nos acostamos a las diez. Pero yo me quedo dormida antes.
dialogue_blocks.translation: We go to bed at ten. But I fall asleep before that.
dialogue_blocks.ipa: [nos akosˈtamos a las ˈðjeθ ‖ ˈpeɾo jo me ˈkeðo doɾˈmiða ˈantes]

exercises.instruction: Escoge la oración correcta (Choose the correct sentence)
exercises.question: How do you say 'She washes her hair every morning'?

options.text: Ella se lava el pelo todas las mañanas.
Ella lava el pelo todas las mañanas.
Ella se lava su pelo todas las mañanas.
Ella te lava el pelo todas las mañanas.

feedback.correct: ¡Exacto! With reflexive verbs, Spanish uses the definite article 'el/la' not a possessive. 'Se lava el pelo' — not 'se lava su pelo'.
feedback.incorrect: The reflexive 'se lava' is needed (she washes herself), and Spanish uses 'el pelo' (the hair) not 'su pelo' when the body part is already understood to be the person's own.

exercises.instruction: Une el verbo con su significado (Match the reflexive verb to its meaning)
Completa con el verbo reflexivo correcto (Complete with the correct reflexive verb)

dialogue.speaker: Carmen
dialogue.text: _____ a las seis. Luego _____ y desayuno. (levantarse / ducharse)

dialogue.speaker: Carmen
dialogue.text: Sí, pero el mayor siempre _____ en el baño. (quedarse)

speaking_rubric.title: Mi rutina diaria (My Daily Routine)
speaking_rubric.scenario: Describe your morning routine from waking up to leaving the house. Use at least 5 reflexive verbs.
speaking_rubric.prompt: Describe tu rutina de la mañana usando verbos reflexivos.

scoring_criteria.description: Daily routine and personal care vocabulary

sample_response.text: Me despierto a las seis y media. Me levanto, me ducho y luego me visto. Me peino rápido y desayuno. Salgo de casa a las siete y cuarto.
sample_response.ipa: [me ðesˈpjeɾto a las ˈsjeis i ˈmeðja ‖ me leˈβanto ‖ me ˈðuʧo i ˈlweɣo me ˈβisto]

cultural_notes.formality: Morning routines are a common conversation topic. Mexicans often greet with '¿Ya te levantaste?' (Are you up already?) as warm small talk with family.
cultural_notes.gestures: Miming washing hands or face while discussing hygiene routines is common in instructional contexts.
cultural_notes.regional_variations: In Mexico, 'bañarse' (to bathe/shower) is used more broadly than 'ducharse' — most people say 'me voy a bañar' regardless of whether they're taking a bath or shower. 'Ducharse' is more common in Spain.
